description: Turn Clicky from a server-proxied app into a shippable personal tool: each user installs the binary, opens a settings window on first launch, pastes their own API keys for Anthropic and/or z.ai (LLM) plus AssemblyAI (streaming transcription) and ElevenLabs (TTS), and Clicky talks directly to those providers from the user's machine. No Cloudflare Worker. No shared backend. No keys on disk in plaintext â€” all secrets encrypted with Windows DPAPI under the current user's profile. The existing worker/ directory remains in the repo because it is shared with the Mac reference implementation, but the Windows client no longer calls it in any code path. Users can switch between Claude and GLM at runtime via a tray menu without restarting. This PRD builds on the completed Windows port (see archive/prd-windows-port.json for the 17 shipped stories) and supersedes the earlier worker-routing GLM integration draft (see archive/prd-glm-worker-routing.json) that this project decided against because it required every user to run Cloudflare Wrangler locally. Target vision model for GLM: glm-4.6v. The Mac reference implementation is not modified.

userStories.title: ILlmClient interface + AnthropicDirectClient (direct to api.anthropic.com)
userStories.description: As a user with my own Anthropic API key, I want Clicky to call api.anthropic.com directly with my key instead of going through a worker, so I can run the app without any backend infrastructure.
acceptanceCriteria: New interface `Clicky.Api.ILlmClient` exposes `IAsyncEnumerable<string> SendAsync(IReadOnlyList<Message> history, IReadOnlyList<CapturedScreen> screens, string systemPrompt, string userText, CancellationToken ct)` â€” the exact surface `CompanionManager` already consumes, extracted from the existing `ClaudeClient`

The existing `Clicky.Api.ClaudeClient` is replaced by `Clicky.Api.AnthropicDirectClient : ILlmClient` that POSTs to `https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages` directly with headers `x-api-key: <userKey>`, `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`, `content-type: application/json`
Constructor signature: `AnthropicDirectClient(string apiKey, string model = "claude-sonnet-4-6", HttpClient? httpClient = null)`. No proxy URL â€” the worker path is gone.

Request body shape, SSE parsing, PNG/JPEG magic-byte media_type detection, and TLS warmup HEAD request behavior are preserved byte-for-byte from the old ClaudeClient (see US-007 notes in archive/progress-windows-port.txt)

`Clicky.Companion.CompanionManager` now takes `ILlmClient` via constructor injection instead of constructing a `ClaudeClient` itself. App.xaml.cs constructs `AnthropicDirectClient` with a temporary hardcoded key for this story only â€” real key plumbing lands in US-019
All existing US-007 tests (SSE parsing, delta extraction, media type detection) pass against the new AnthropicDirectClient unchanged
One new test: a `FakeLlmClient : ILlmClient` verifies CompanionManager's dependency injection seam works end-to-end

userStories.title: SecretsStore (DPAPI) + SettingsStore (JSON) under %APPDATA%\Clicky
userStories.description: As a user, I want my API keys encrypted at rest using my Windows account credentials, and my non-secret settings persisted in a readable JSON file, so nobody can extract my keys from disk and I can inspect/reset my config without a GUI if needed.
acceptanceCriteria: `Clicky.Companion.SecretsStore` wraps `System.Security.Cryptography.ProtectedData.Protect` / `Unprotect` with `DataProtectionScope.CurrentUser`. Methods: `void Write(string key, string value)`, `string? Read(string key)`, `void Delete(string key)`, `bool Exists(string key)`
Secrets are stored as an encrypted blob at `%APPDATA%\Clicky\secrets.bin`. The plaintext internal format is a simple JSON dictionary; encryption is applied to the whole file, not per-entry

`Clicky.Companion.SettingsStore` reads/writes `%APPDATA%\Clicky\settings.json` with fields: `LlmProvider` (string: "anthropic" or "zai"), `LlmModel` (string), `ElevenLabsVoiceId` (string, default `kPzsL2i3teMYv0FxEYQ6` to match Mac), `OnboardingComplete` (bool), `AnalyticsOptOut` (bool)
Both stores auto-create the %APPDATA%\Clicky directory on first write
Keys used by SecretsStore are standardized constants: `anthropic_api_key`, `zai_api_key`, `assemblyai_api_key`, `elevenlabs_api_key`

App.xaml.cs on OnStartup instantiates SettingsStore + SecretsStore, reads the configured provider/model/keys, and passes them into the CompanionManager constructor (replacing the hardcoded key from US-018)

US-014's OnboardingService registry read is migrated: if `HKCU\Software\Clicky\onboarded` exists, copy its value to SettingsStore.OnboardingComplete on first run of this story's code, then prefer the SettingsStore value thereafter. Same for `analyticsOptOut`

Unit tests: SecretsStore round-trip (write â†’ read â†’ same value), SecretsStore decryption under a different user profile fails gracefully (returns null rather than throwing), SettingsStore round-trip, SettingsStore defaults when file is missing, migration from registry test
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass on a Windows test host
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-019 - SecretsStore (DPAPI) + SettingsStore under APPDATA
userStories.notes: DPAPI is per-user-account, not per-machine â€” backups restored to a different Windows user will fail to decrypt, which is correct security behavior. Document this in the SettingsWindow help text (lands in US-022). Do NOT add a passphrase fallback; the whole point of DPAPI is that it's invisible to the user.

userStories.title: ZaiDirectClient for GLM models (direct to api.z.ai OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
userStories.description: As a user with a z.ai API key, I want Clicky to use GLM-4.6V as a vision-capable alternative to Claude, with the same UI and screen-pointing behavior, so I have a choice of LLM providers.
acceptanceCriteria: New `Clicky.Api.ZaiDirectClient : ILlmClient` POSTs to `https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4/chat/completions` with headers `Authorization: Bearer <userKey>`, `Content-Type: application/json`, `Accept: text/event-stream`
Constructor: `ZaiDirectClient(string apiKey, string model = "glm-4.6v", HttpClient? httpClient = null)`

Translates Clicky's existing Anthropic-shape inputs (system prompt + message history + CapturedScreen list + user text) into OpenAI chat-completions request body: system prompt becomes `messages[0] = {role:"system", content: systemPrompt}`, history turns pass through with their existing roles, the final user turn's text + screens become one user message with a `content` array of `{type:"text", text:...}` and `{type:"image_url", image_url:{url:"data:<media_type>;base64,<base64Data>"}}` entries
Image media_type is sniffed by PNG/JPEG magic bytes (reuse `AnthropicDirectClient.DetectImageMediaType` via refactor or duplicate â€” do not import a new library)

Streaming response parsing: reads `data: {...}` SSE lines, extracts `choices[0].delta.content` text chunks, yields them through the `IAsyncEnumerable<string>` return (same interface shape as AnthropicDirectClient). The `[DONE]` sentinel ends the stream cleanly
Chunk-boundary UTF-8 splits (a multi-byte character split across two HTTP chunks) do not corrupt the yielded text â€” use an incremental decoder, not a per-chunk Encoding.UTF8.GetString
Malformed JSON lines are logged and skipped, not thrown â€” one bad line must not kill the stream

Valid model values accepted: `glm-4.6v` (default, vision), `glm-4.5v` (fallback, vision). Text-only models (`glm-5.1`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5`, `glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.7-no-vision-variants`) must NOT be offered as choices because they silently break the [POINT:x,y:label:screenN] feature â€” Clicky requires vision

Unit tests: text-only request translation, single-image request translation, multi-image multi-screen request translation, SSE chunk parsing fixture (realistic glm-4.6v response with a [POINT:...] tag mid-stream), UTF-8 chunk-boundary split test, malformed-chunk skip test
End-to-end test with FakeHttpMessageHandler: canned OpenAI SSE input â†’ expected string deltas out through ILlmClient.SendAsync
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-020 - ZaiDirectClient for GLM-4.6V
userStories.notes: This is the most algorithmically dense story. Budget time for the SSE chunk-boundary tests â€” getting them right is what separates a demo-quality integration from a shippable one. The Anthropic-shape system prompt (built by CompanionManager in US-010, includes the [POINT:x,y:label:screenN] tag instructions) passes through unchanged â€” GLM-4.6V is prompted in the same English, it just speaks OpenAI over the wire.

userStories.title: Quit reliably terminates the process
userStories.description: As a user, when I click Quit from the tray menu I want the app to actually exit â€” no lingering Clicky.App.exe in Task Manager, no orphaned WASAPI / WebSocket / HttpClient threads keeping the process alive.
acceptanceCriteria: Reproduce the bug first: launch Clicky, do at least one push-to-talk turn (so mic capture + transcription WebSocket + LLM stream + TTS playback have all run), then click tray â†’ Quit. Confirm Clicky.App.exe is still in Task Manager. Capture a managed-thread dump (procdump -ma, or debugger attach) BEFORE writing any fix so the culprit thread is identified, not guessed

Root-cause the hang. Likely suspects, in order: (a) `CompanionManager.Dispose` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/CompanionManager.cs:502-514` cancels `_hookConsumerCts` but never awaits the fire-and-forget `ConsumeHotkeyTransitionsAsync` task started at line 110; (b) `MicrophoneCapture.Dispose` at `windows/src/Clicky.Audio/MicrophoneCapture.cs:150-155` may not join the NAudio WASAPI thread; (c) HTTP calls inside `AnthropicDirectClient`, `ZaiDirectClient`, `ElevenLabsTtsClient`, `AssemblyAiStreamingTranscriber` may be running without a shutdown CancellationToken and blocking the dispatcher. Document in progress.txt which one(s) were actually holding the process alive

Fix the identified root cause(s). Required changes: store long-running background tasks as `Task` fields (not `_ = ...`) and `await` them with a 2-second timeout inside the owner's `Dispose`/`DisposeAsync`. Any new worker `Thread` must be `IsBackground = true`. Every outbound `HttpClient.SendAsync` / `ClientWebSocket.ReceiveAsync` on the shutdown path must accept a `CancellationToken` wired to a shutdown `CancellationTokenSource` owned by `App`

`App.xaml.cs` gains a shutdown `CancellationTokenSource` created in `OnStartup`, cancelled at the top of `OnExit` before any Dispose calls, and passed into CompanionManager/clients through their existing constructors (or a new `SetShutdownToken` method if adding a ctor param ripples too widely)

`App.OnExit` at `windows/src/Clicky.App/App.xaml.cs:427-459` is converted to dispose in a deterministic order: (1) cancel shutdown token, (2) await CompanionManager.DisposeAsync with 2s timeout, (3) dispose overlay manager, (4) dispose push-to-talk hook, (5) dispose tray icon. Add a final safety net: after `base.OnExit(e)`, if any foreground thread is still alive after 500ms, call `Environment.Exit(0)`. The safety net is belt-and-braces â€” it must NOT be used to paper over a hang the dispose chain should have prevented

Unit test: a `FakeLlmClient` and `FakeTranscriber` that intentionally block forever on an `HttpClient`-like stream. `CompanionManager.DisposeAsync` must still return within 2 seconds by cancelling them. Same pattern for `MicrophoneCapture`

Manual verification: after the fix, perform the original repro (one full push-to-talk turn, then Quit). Task Manager must show Clicky.App.exe gone within 1 second of the click. Repeat 3Ã— â€” all three must terminate cleanly
Regression check: Quit from a cold state (app just started, no turn performed) must also work. Quit mid-turn (while TTS is speaking) must cancel playback AND exit within 1 second
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Append diagnosis + fix summary to progress.txt, including the exact thread stack(s) that were blocking shutdown
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-026 - Quit reliably terminates the process
userStories.notes: Diagnose before fixing. `Environment.Exit(0)` is a hammer that will hide bugs â€” fix the actual blocker first, then add the safety net. The `ShutdownMode="OnExplicitShutdown"` in App.xaml is correct for a tray app and must not be changed; any non-background foreground thread will prevent process exit under that mode, which is why hunting down the specific blocker matters.

userStories.title: Fix DPI-scaling bug that breaks pointer accuracy on high-DPI displays
userStories.description: As a user on a high-DPI Windows laptop, when I ask Clicky to point at something on screen I want the blue cursor to land on the actual target, not 20â€“50% off. On scaled displays (125%, 150%, 200%) the pointer currently lands far from the intended element, and the error grows with distance from the monitor origin.
acceptanceCriteria: Reproduce the bug on a 150% DPI display first. Ask Clicky to point at a well-known target (Windows taskbar clock, a specific file in the VS Code explorer). Document the pixel offset and its scaling behavior in progress.txt before fixing

Root cause: physical pixels are being handed to WPF APIs that expect DIPs. Three files are implicated: `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:425-437` returns monitor bounds from `GetMonitorInfo` in physical pixels; `windows/src/Clicky.Pointing/PointTagParser.cs:94-103` produces a `screenPoint` in physical pixels by scaling and offsetting with those bounds; `windows/src/Clicky.Overlay/OverlayWindow.cs:41-48` assigns those physical-pixel values directly to WPF `Window.Left/Top/Width/Height` which are always DIPs (the comment on line 41-43 asserting otherwise is wrong); `windows/src/Clicky.Overlay/BlueCursorControl.cs:197-198` then subtracts physical-pixel overlay bounds from a physical-pixel target to compute canvas-local coordinates, which WPF also interprets as DIPs

Fix strategy: keep screenshotâ†’physical-pixel math in the capture/parser layer, and convert to DIPs exactly once at the WPF boundary. Introduce a `DpiHelper` (or similar) that calls `GetDpiForMonitor(MDT_EFFECTIVE_DPI)` per `HMONITOR` and returns a `(double ScaleX, double ScaleY)` tuple, plus a convenience `System.Windows.Point ToDips(System.Drawing.Point physical, HMONITOR monitor)`

`OverlayWindow` constructor converts `monitorBounds` from physical pixels to DIPs before assigning `Left/Top/Width/Height`. Store both the original physical `MonitorBounds` (for matching against PointDirective.DisplayBounds) AND the new DIP bounds (for layout). The comment on line 41-43 is deleted and replaced with one that correctly describes WPF's DIP coordinate system

`BlueCursorControl.FlyTo` converts `targetPoint` and the `GetCursorPos` result from physical pixels to DIPs before computing canvas-local coordinates. Uses the overlay's cached DPI scale to avoid re-querying the OS on every animation tick

`PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinates` is NOT changed â€” it continues to return physical-pixel global coordinates. The DIP conversion happens at the overlay-layer boundary, giving a single conversion point that future code can trust

Unit test: `DpiHelper` round-trip (physical â†’ DIP â†’ physical) is identity for a monitor at 100%, and scales correctly for 125% / 150% / 200%. Provide a fake monitor-DPI source so tests don't depend on the real display config
Unit test: `PointTagParser` output is unchanged (no regression in the existing physical-pixel contract)
Unit test: a test `OverlayWindow` (or extracted helper) given a physical-pixel target on a 150% monitor produces a DIP canvas-local point matching the expected value within 0.5 DIP

Manual verification on a high-DPI laptop: hotkey â†’ "point at the Windows clock" â†’ the blue cursor must land on the clock, not beside it. Repeat on the far corner of the screen (where the DPI bug causes the largest offset) â€” must still land within ~20 px of the target. If multi-monitor is available, test the same on a secondary monitor at a different DPI
Regression check on a 100% single-monitor setup: accuracy must be unchanged from before the fix
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Append the reproduced offset values (before) and the post-fix measurements (after) to progress.txt
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-028 - Fix DPI scaling for pointer accuracy on high-DPI displays
userStories.notes: The secondary hypothesis â€” that the image dimensions sent to Claude (AnthropicDirectClient.cs:63-76) disagree with the resized JPEG dimensions â€” should only be investigated if DPI alone doesn't close the gap. Measure before and after. Do NOT change the coordinate contract of PointTagParser; keeping it in physical pixels means future code (e.g. a hypothetical Win32 SendInput click feature) still has the right reference frame.

userStories.title: Stream TTS + overlap capture to cut perceived latency
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to start speaking almost as soon as I let go of the push-to-talk key, not after several seconds of silence. Today, TTS only starts playing after the whole ElevenLabs MP3 has been downloaded AND the entire LLM response has streamed â€” both are serialized pointlessly.
acceptanceCriteria: Measure current latency as a baseline before changing anything. Instrument `CompanionManager.SendTranscriptToClaudeWithScreenshot` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/CompanionManager.cs:318-396` to log (a) key-releaseâ†’first-LLM-token, (b) first-LLM-tokenâ†’first-TTS-byte, (c) first-TTS-byteâ†’first-audio-heard. Record the baseline numbers in progress.txt

Change 1 â€” stream TTS audio instead of buffering the whole MP3. Update `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs:62-92` to consume the HTTP response as a `Stream` and feed it into NAudio via a buffered pipe (`MediaFoundationReader` over a `BufferedWaveProvider`, or a custom `Stream` wrapper that blocks `Read` until more bytes arrive). First audio sample must leave the speakers no later than ~300 ms after the first HTTP response byte arrives, not after the full MP3 is downloaded

Change 2 â€” start TTS on sentence boundaries instead of waiting for the whole LLM response. In `CompanionManager` where the LLM `IAsyncEnumerable<string>` is consumed, buffer tokens until the first sentence terminator (`.`, `!`, `?`, or two consecutive newlines), kick off an ElevenLabs TTS request for that chunk, continue buffering the next sentence, and queue subsequent TTS requests to play sequentially without gaps. The `[POINT:x,y:label:screenN]` tag must still be stripped before the text is spoken (`PointTagParser.Parse` already handles this â€” call it per-sentence)

Change 3 â€” overlap screen capture with the hotkey press. Today capture runs after key release. Kick off `ScreenCapture.CaptureAllScreensAsJpegAsync` when the key is first pressed (inside the hotkey press handler in `CompanionManager`), store the `Task<List<CapturedScreen>>`, and `await` it at the point the LLM request is built. On key release the capture is usually already done, saving 300â€“1000 ms. Cancel the capture task if the press is shorter than ~100 ms (accidental tap)

Change 4 â€” tighten the WGC first-frame timeout at `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:171` from 2 seconds to 800 ms. On Windows 11, WGC first-frame is normally <300 ms; the 2 s ceiling just prolongs failure fallback to GDI+

Change 5 (optional, only if time permits) â€” pre-warm HTTPS connections to `api.anthropic.com`, `api.z.ai`, `api.elevenlabs.io`, and `streaming.assemblyai.com` on `App.OnStartup` with a throwaway HEAD or GET. Skip with a TODO if it adds complexity

Cancellation: every new async path (pipelined TTS queue, capture-on-press, streamed audio reader) must honor `CompanionManager`'s existing cancellation plumbing from US-010. A new push-to-talk must cleanly cancel any in-flight TTS playback and queued sentences, matching the existing behavior for full-response TTS

Ordering guarantee: sentences must play in the order they were emitted by the LLM. Test with a mocked LLM that emits sentences at varying speeds and a mocked TTS client with variable latency â€” the audio queue must never reorder

Unit tests: sentence-boundary tokenizer (handles `[POINT:...]` tag stripped mid-sentence, handles newlines, handles abbreviations like `Dr.` or `e.g.` gracefully â€” accept some false positives but document them), streaming MP3 reader starts playback before stream completes, pipelined TTS queue preserves order under varying latency, capture-on-press cancellation on short tap

Manual measurement after the fix: the same three latency metrics from the baseline, with each change applied incrementally. Target: end-to-end key-releaseâ†’first-audio-heard under 1.5 seconds on a typical broadband connection (baseline today is likely 3â€“6 s). Document actual before/after numbers in progress.txt
Regression check: pointing accuracy from US-028 must be unchanged â€” the capture-on-press change must still hand Claude the correct screenshot dimensions and `CapturedScreen.DisplayBounds`
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-029 - Stream TTS and overlap capture for perceived latency
userStories.notes: Ship changes 1+2+3 as the core of this story â€” they compound. Change 4 is a trivial one-line win. Change 5 is optional. Sentence-boundary detection is a known tar pit; accept a simple `.!?` + newline heuristic, do not pull in a sentence-tokenizer library, and do not attempt to handle edge cases like `Dr. Smith` perfectly â€” if a false split causes a short extra TTS call, that is acceptable. Measure, don't guess: the baseline and post-fix numbers in progress.txt are the acceptance gate, not code review aesthetics.

userStories.title: Higher-resolution screenshots for better pointer accuracy on all displays
userStories.description: As a user on any display resolution, I want Clicky to send higher-resolution screenshots to the LLM so that the [POINT:x,y] coordinates it returns are more precise, and the blue cursor lands closer to the intended UI element.
acceptanceCriteria: Increase `MaxDimension` from 1280 to 2048 in `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:20`. This halves the coordinate error on 2560px displays and reduces it by 3Ã— on 4K (3840px) displays â€” every pixel of LLM imprecision now maps to fewer physical pixels

Lower `JpegQuality` from 80 to 75 in `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:21` to partially offset the larger image payload. At 2048px the JPEG is about 40-60% larger at quality 75 vs 1280px at quality 80, which is acceptable for broadband connections and stays well within Claude and GLM-4.6V's image size limits

Replace `(int)` truncation with `(int)Math.Round(...)` in `CalculateScaledSize` at `windows/src/Clicky.Capture/ScreenCapture.cs:377-378`. Current code `(int)(MaxDimension / aspect)` truncates fractional pixels â€” on ultrawide displays (e.g. 3440x1440, aspect 2.388) this causes a 1-pixel dimension mismatch between the reported `ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` and the actual JPEG, which accumulates into a pointing offset at screen edges

Fix off-by-one clamping in `PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinates` at `windows/src/Clicky.Pointing/PointTagParser.cs:89-90`. Change `Math.Min(directive.X, target.ScreenshotPixelWidth)` to `Math.Min(directive.X, target.ScreenshotPixelWidth - 1)` and same for Y â€” coordinates are 0-indexed so the max valid coordinate is `dimension - 1`, not `dimension`
Update any test fixtures that assert `MaxDimension == 1280` to expect 2048. Grep for `1280` in `windows/tests/` to find them

These changes must improve accuracy on ALL display configurations, not just high-DPI: (a) standard 1920Ã—1080 at 100% â€” screenshot is 1920Ã—1080 (no resize needed, within 2048), improving precision from 1.5:1 to 1:1 compared to the old 1280px cap; (b) 2560Ã—1440 at 100% â€” screenshot is 2048Ã—1152 (1.25:1 instead of 2:1); (c) 3840Ã—2160 at 150% â€” physical capture is 3840Ã—2160, resized to 2048Ã—1152 (1.875:1 instead of 3:1); (d) 3440Ã—1440 ultrawide â€” resized to 2048Ã—857 (1.68:1 instead of 2.69:1)

Regression: existing US-028 DPI tests and US-029 capture-on-press tests must still pass. The `ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` metadata stored in `CapturedScreen` must still match the actual JPEG dimensions exactly â€” verify with a unit test that captures a known-size bitmap and checks the metadata
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-030 - Higher-resolution screenshots for pointer accuracy
userStories.notes: This is the single highest-impact change for pointing accuracy. At 1280px, Claude is routinely off by 10-20 screenshot pixels, which maps to 20-60 physical pixels depending on the display. At 2048px the same 10-20 pixel error maps to 12-37 physical pixels â€” a 40-50% improvement with zero changes to the LLM prompting. The trade-off is slightly larger API payloads (~150KB vs ~100KB per screenshot), which adds 50-100ms on broadband but is negligible compared to the 500-2000ms LLM TTFT. Do NOT go above 2048 â€” larger images don't measurably improve vision model coordinate accuracy but do increase cost and latency.

userStories.title: Eager first-chunk TTS to cut time-to-first-audio
userStories.description: As a user, I want to hear the beginning of Clicky's response as fast as possible after I release the push-to-talk key, instead of waiting for the LLM to produce a complete sentence ending with a period before TTS starts.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an eager first-chunk mode to `SentenceTokenizer` at `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/SentenceTokenizer.cs`. Add a private `bool _firstChunkEmitted = false` field. When `_firstChunkEmitted` is false and the buffer has accumulated at least 60 characters, `FindSentenceBoundary` additionally considers `,` (comma), `â€”` (em-dash, U+2014), `â€“` (en-dash, U+2013), `;` (semicolon), and `:` (colon) followed by a space as valid split points, in addition to the existing `.!?\n\n` boundaries

After the first chunk is emitted via the eager path, set `_firstChunkEmitted = true` and revert to the standard `.!?\n\n` sentence-boundary logic for all subsequent chunks â€” this prevents choppy playback after the initial response
The 60-character minimum prevents pathological splits on very short opening phrases like `yeah,` or `sure,` â€” these should buffer until a normal sentence boundary or the eager threshold is hit

Example: Claude streams `you'll want to open the color inspector â€” it's right up in the top right area of the toolbar.` The old tokenizer waits for the final `.` (entire sentence). The new tokenizer emits `you'll want to open the color inspector` at the em-dash after ~45 tokens, then `it's right up in the top right area of the toolbar.` at the period. Estimated savings: 500-1500ms off first-audio latency depending on LLM token rate
Edge case: if the first `.!?` arrives before the buffer hits 60 chars, the normal sentence boundary fires first and `_firstChunkEmitted` is set â€” no double-split risk
Edge case: [POINT:...] tags must not be counted toward the 60-char threshold. Strip point tags from the length check (not from the actual text â€” stripping happens downstream in CompanionManager)

Unit tests: (a) eager split on comma after 60+ chars, (b) eager split on em-dash, (c) no eager split when buffer < 60 chars (waits for normal boundary), (d) normal `.!?` boundary still fires if it comes before 60 chars, (e) second sentence uses normal boundaries only (no mid-comma splits), (f) `[POINT:...]` tag not counted in threshold
This improvement works identically on all hardware â€” it is pure text-processing logic with no OS, DPI, or device dependencies
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-031 - Eager first-chunk TTS splitting
userStories.notes: This pairs with US-032 (streaming MP3 playback). Together they cut 800-2300ms from first-audio latency. Alone, this saves 500-1500ms because the TTS request fires earlier. Do NOT reduce the 60-char threshold below 50 â€” very short TTS chunks sound robotic and are wasteful (ElevenLabs has per-request overhead). Do NOT add clause-level splitting for sentences after the first â€” it makes the voice choppy. The first sentence is the only one where latency matters more than naturalness.

userStories.title: True streaming MP3 playback for TTS
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to start playing the TTS audio as soon as the first MP3 frames arrive from ElevenLabs, instead of waiting for the entire MP3 file to download before playback begins.
acceptanceCriteria: Rewrite `PlayStreamingMp3Async` in `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs:98-157`. Currently the method downloads the entire MP3 into a `MemoryStream`, then creates an `Mp3FileReader` and plays. The new implementation must start playback after the first few MP3 frames are decoded, while the HTTP download continues in parallel

Implementation approach: (a) create a `BufferedWaveProvider` at 44100 Hz stereo (or whatever format the first `Mp3Frame` reports) with a buffer of ~5 seconds; (b) start `WaveOutEvent`/`WasapiOut` playing from the `BufferedWaveProvider` immediately; (c) in a loop, read chunks from the HTTP response stream, decode MP3 frames using `Mp3Frame.LoadFromStream` + `IMp3FrameDecompressor` (NAudio's `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor`), and add decoded PCM to the `BufferedWaveProvider`; (d) when the HTTP stream ends, wait for the `BufferedWaveProvider` to drain (playback completes)

The `BufferedWaveProvider.ReadFully` property must be set to `true` so it returns silence instead of stalling when the HTTP stream is temporarily slower than playback â€” this prevents audio glitches on slow connections
Handle format detection: the first `Mp3Frame` determines the sample rate and channel count. Create the `BufferedWaveProvider` and `IMp3FrameDecompressor` after the first frame is decoded, not before

Preserve the existing `StopPlayback()` semantics: calling `StopPlayback()` mid-stream must (a) stop the `WaveOutEvent`/`WasapiOut`, (b) cancel the HTTP download loop, and (c) dispose all resources. Use the existing `_lock` for thread safety
Preserve the existing `IsPlaying` property: return true while the `WaveOutEvent` is in `PlaybackState.Playing`

Preserve the WasapiOut path for specific output devices (`_outputDeviceId`): the `CreateWavePlayer` helper is unchanged, the only difference is what `IWaveProvider` is passed to `Init()` â€” `BufferedWaveProvider` instead of `Mp3FileReader`

Estimated latency savings: 300-800ms per TTS sentence. The old path waited for the full MP3 download (typically 300-800ms for a 1-2 sentence chunk at ElevenLabs' output rate). The new path starts playback after ~2-3 MP3 frames (~50-100ms of audio data)

This works on all Windows machines â€” NAudio's `Mp3Frame` and `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor` use the ACM codec that ships with every Windows installation since Vista. No additional codec or library is required

Unit test: mock HTTP stream that feeds MP3 data in 1KB chunks with 50ms delays. Verify `IsPlaying` becomes true before all chunks are delivered (proving playback started during download). Use a real short MP3 fixture (generate one with NAudio or include a ~1s test file)
Cancellation test: start streaming playback, cancel after 200ms â€” verify no resource leaks (all disposed within 1s)
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-032 - True streaming MP3 playback for TTS
userStories.notes: The current `PlayStreamingMp3Async` already has the scaffolding for streaming (the `playbackStarted` flag, the `startPlaybackThreshold` constant) but the comment at line 126-128 says 'For simplicity we'll wait for the full download since NAudio's Mp3FileReader needs seekable streams.' The fix is to bypass `Mp3FileReader` entirely and decode frame-by-frame with `Mp3Frame` + `AcmMp3FrameDecompressor` into a `BufferedWaveProvider`. This is a well-documented NAudio pattern. Do NOT pull in MediaFoundation or any external codec â€” the built-in ACM MP3 decoder is universally available and sufficient.

userStories.description: As a user, I want the first TTS request to ElevenLabs to be as fast as subsequent ones, by pre-establishing the TLS connection at app startup instead of paying the handshake cost on the first push-to-talk turn.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a `WarmUpTlsConnectionIfNeeded` method to `ElevenLabsTtsClient` at `windows/src/Clicky.Api/ElevenLabsTtsClient.cs`, following the exact same pattern as `AnthropicDirectClient.WarmUpTlsConnectionIfNeeded` at `windows/src/Clicky.Api/AnthropicDirectClient.cs:182-204`

Failures are silently swallowed (try/catch around the whole warmup) â€” this is purely an optimization, not a requirement. A failed warmup just means the first TTS call pays the full TLS handshake
Estimated savings: 100-300ms on the first TTS request. Subsequent requests reuse the pooled connection regardless
This works on all networks and all Windows configurations â€” it's a standard HTTP optimization with no OS-specific dependencies

Commit all changes with message: feat: US-039 - Only send cursor screen to LLM
userStories.notes: The Mac version sends all screens and it works because Claude is good at multi-image reasoning. GLM-4v via z.ai is not â€” debug logs show it picks coordinates from the wrong image or gets confused by the multi-screen labels. Filtering to one screen eliminates this ambiguity entirely. The capture still enumerates all monitors (needed for sorting and cursor detection) but only the cursor screen's image is sent to the LLM.

userStories.title: Send full-resolution screenshots (remove 1280px downscale)
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to send my screen to the LLM at full native resolution instead of downscaling to 1280px, so the vision model can see fine UI details (small icons, tab buttons, text) and return more accurate pointing coordinates.
acceptanceCriteria: In `ScreenCapture.cs`, change `MaxDimension` from `1280` to `3840` â€” this preserves native resolution for 1080p and 1440p monitors while still capping 4K displays to avoid excessively large payloads
The `CalculateScaledSize` method is unchanged â€” it already handles any `MaxDimension` value correctly
Both WGC and GDI capture paths continue to work (the scaling logic is downstream of capture and works for any dimension)
The coordinate conversion in `PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinates` continues to work because it uses `ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` and `DisplayBounds` which are always self-consistent
For a 1920x1080 monitor, the screenshot sent to the LLM should now be 1920x1080 (not 1280x720). Verify via debug log: `[POINT] capture:` line should show `screenshot=1920x1080`
Estimated payload increase: ~2-3x larger JPEG base64. Acceptable trade-off for accuracy.
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-040 - Send full-resolution screenshots to LLM
userStories.notes: At 1280px max edge, a 1920x1080 monitor is downscaled to 1280x720. Small UI elements like the browser + tab button, toolbar icons, and menu items lose detail. The vision model (GLM-4v) struggles to distinguish nearby elements at this resolution. Sending native res gives the model the same visual fidelity a human would have. The Mac version also uses 1280 but works because Claude's vision is more capable. This is the simplest change with the highest expected impact for weaker vision models.

userStories.title: Draw coordinate grid overlay on screenshots for LLM spatial anchoring
userStories.description: As a user, I want Clicky to draw a subtle coordinate grid on screenshots before sending them to the LLM, so the vision model has visual spatial anchors and can return more precise pixel coordinates.
acceptanceCriteria: New static method `ScreenCapture.DrawCoordinateGrid(Bitmap bitmap)` draws a grid overlay on a GDI+ bitmap in-place
Grid lines every 200 pixels (both horizontal and vertical), drawn in semi-transparent gray (e.g. `Color.FromArgb(80, 180, 180, 180)`) with 1px pen width
At each grid intersection, draw the coordinate label (e.g. `200,400`) in a small readable font (9pt, sans-serif), white text on a small dark semi-transparent background rectangle for contrast
Labels are positioned slightly offset from the intersection point so they don't overlap with the crosshair

In the GDI capture path (`CaptureWithGdi`): call `DrawCoordinateGrid(fullBitmap)` after `CopyFromScreen` but before the resize step â€” the grid is drawn at native resolution and gets resized with the image

In the WGC capture path (`CaptureSingleFrameAsync`): after converting the `SoftwareBitmap` to JPEG, the grid cannot easily be drawn on WinRT bitmaps. Instead, add an alternative: decode the JPEG back to a GDI+ `Bitmap`, draw the grid, re-encode to JPEG. OR: convert the `SoftwareBitmap` to a GDI+ `Bitmap` before encoding, draw the grid, then encode. Choose whichever is simpler.

The grid must NOT be drawn when capturing for `DumpToTempAsync` test output â€” add an optional `bool drawGrid = false` parameter to the internal capture methods, and only pass `true` from the production pipeline in `CompanionManager`
Verify visually: use tray menu 'Test Overlay' â†’ check `%TEMP%\clicky-capture\` screenshots to confirm grid is visible, readable, and not too intrusive
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: feat: US-041 - Coordinate grid overlay on screenshots
userStories.notes: Vision models that aren't specifically trained for pixel-coordinate tasks (like GLM-4v) benefit significantly from visual coordinate anchors. The grid gives the model reference points it can count from rather than guessing absolute positions. 200px spacing gives ~10 reference points across a 1920px screen â€” enough granularity without visual clutter. The grid should be subtle enough not to obscure UI elements but visible enough for the model to read. This is a well-known technique from computer-use and GUI-agent research.

userStories.title: Deterministic end-to-end pointing fixture tests with labeled boxes
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a deterministic test harness that renders synthetic screenshots with obvious labeled boxes like box A, box B, and box C, then runs fake `[POINT:x,y:label:screenN]` responses through the real Windows pointing pipeline, so regressions in screenshot-space to display-space mapping are caught in CI without requiring manual testing.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a new test file under `windows/tests/Clicky.Tests/` that builds synthetic JPEG screenshots in memory containing multiple clearly labeled colored boxes (for example `box a`, `box b`, `box c`) at known screenshot-space coordinates

The tests must exercise the real Windows parser and conversion path â€” `PointTagParser.Parse()` followed by `PointTagParser.ConvertToScreenCoordinatesDetailed()` â€” rather than duplicating the math inline in the test

For each synthetic fixture, the final mapped `ScreenPoint` must be asserted to land inside the expected display-space box bounds, not merely equal one hardcoded center pixel, so the tests tolerate harmless rounding but fail on meaningful misalignment

Cover at least these monitor/layout cases: single monitor at 100% scaling, single monitor at high DPI (150% or equivalent), a secondary monitor with a non-zero or negative virtual-desktop origin, and an explicit `:screen2` directive that proves the correct display is selected

Also assert the screenshot metadata contract remains valid: `CapturedScreen.ScreenshotPixelWidth/Height` must match the actual encoded JPEG dimensions exactly, because any drift there corrupts the coordinate space the LLM is told to use
The suite must be self-contained and require no live desktop, no real monitor, no API keys, and no manual interaction â€” it must run inside the normal `dotnet test` flow
Build: 0 warnings. Tests: all pass
Commit all changes with message: test: US-042 - Deterministic end-to-end pointing fixture tests
userStories.notes: This is the safe automation layer for the pointing pipeline. It does not prove that WPF physically drew the cursor on the right pixel of a real monitor, but it does prove that the screenshot-space, display-space, multi-monitor-selection, and metadata contracts are stable. Keep the fixtures obvious and labeled so future debugging artifacts are easy to reason about.

userStories.title: One-command desktop pointing smoke harness with artifact capture
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a one-command local smoke harness that exercises the live Windows overlay against a real desktop target and saves artifacts automatically, so I can verify that saying things like 'show me where box A is' lands on the correct on-screen element without manually stitching together logs and screenshots.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a local-only smoke-test workflow, script, or tray-triggered diagnostic path that presents a deterministic on-screen fixture with clearly labeled target boxes and then drives the existing capture → parse → convert → fly pipeline against that fixture

The harness must save or surface the same core evidence automatically for each run: the raw screenshot sent to the model or fake-point path, an annotated screenshot showing the chosen pixel, and the corresponding `%APPDATA%\Clicky\debug.log` entries for parse/conversion/overlay dispatch

The workflow must let a developer verify two separate questions quickly: (1) did the chosen screenshot-space point land inside the intended box in the captured image, and (2) did the live blue cursor land on the same box on the real screen
The harness must support the monitor cases most likely to break Windows coordinate math: 100% DPI, high-DPI single monitor, and mixed/secondary monitor setups when available
Keep this explicitly outside normal CI if it depends on a real desktop compositor or visible WPF windows; the goal is a reliable local verification tool, not a flaky headless UI test
Document the invocation path in `progress.txt` or a nearby developer note so future debugging sessions can use it without rediscovering the workflow
Build: 0 warnings. Tests and smoke path both pass

userStories.notes: US-042 covers the math in a hermetic environment; this story covers the last mile on a real Windows desktop. Keep the scope pragmatic: artifact-driven verification is more valuable than trying to fully automate a fragile visual assertion against the compositor.

userStories.title: Reliable end-to-end test coverage for the full Clicky turn
userStories.description: As a developer, I want proper end-to-end tests that exercise a realistic full Clicky turn and fail for real regressions instead of test flakiness, so I can trust changes to capture, transcription, LLM streaming, TTS, and pointing before shipping.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an explicit test pyramid for the Windows app: fast unit tests, deterministic end-to-end integration tests that run in normal `dotnet test`, and a separate desktop-only smoke test path for compositor-dependent validation

Create at least one deterministic full-turn end-to-end test that wires together the real `CompanionManager` orchestration with fake implementations of the external boundaries (`ILlmClient`, transcription input, TTS client, screen capture provider, overlay dispatcher as needed) so the test covers the actual application flow rather than isolated helper methods

The deterministic end-to-end test must verify the core turn sequence in order: push-to-talk start, transcript accumulation/finalization, screenshot capture, LLM streaming response consumption, `[POINT:x,y:label:screenN]` parsing when present, overlay dispatch, TTS kickoff, and return to `Idle` state

The end-to-end suite must assert observable outcomes, not just absence of exceptions: emitted response text, final voice state transitions, whether overlay dispatch was requested, whether TTS was started, and whether user-facing error state is set correctly on failure paths

Cover at least these failure cases with deterministic tests: missing or invalid key path re-opens Settings / surfaces actionable error, malformed point tag does not crash the turn, LLM stream cancellation during a model swap or re-press returns the app to a clean state, and TTS/transcription failures surface friendly errors without wedging the session

Any tests that require a real Windows desktop, WPF compositor, tray icon, or visible overlay must remain outside the default CI path and be invoked through the local smoke harness from `US-043`; `dotnet test` must stay reliable on a normal Windows test host
Document which tests are CI-safe versus desktop-only, how to run both, and what evidence a developer should inspect when a desktop smoke test fails
Build: 0 warnings. `dotnet test` passes reliably across repeated runs. Desktop smoke verification path documented and working
Commit all changes with message: test: US-044 - Reliable end-to-end coverage for full Clicky turn
userStories.notes: This story is about trustworthiness, not just more tests. Prefer a small number of high-signal end-to-end tests over broad but brittle UI automation. Keep real-desktop assertions in the local smoke layer, and keep CI on deterministic seams you control.

userStories.title: Reset Windows pointing architecture by deleting failed layered implementations
userStories.description: As a user who has seen repeated wrong pointing on maps and slow visible cursor timing, I want the Windows pointing stack reset to a small, understandable baseline before any new solution is attempted, so future work is not built on verifier/locator/grid/debug layers that already failed in real use.
acceptanceCriteria: Remove the failed point-location layers from `windows/src/Clicky.Companion/CompanionManager.cs`: no second-pass verifier, no fast point-locator side request, no duplicate/fallback point dispatch races, no prompt-only coordinate correction scaffolding, and no hidden LLM call whose only job is to reinterpret the same screenshot

Remove or quarantine tests that encode the failed behavior as success, especially tests that assert a second LLM call corrects a bad coordinate, or that treat synthetic labeled boxes as proof of real-world semantic pointing accuracy

Keep only the proven coordinate contract and last-mile renderer baseline: `PointTagParser` parses `[POINT:x,y:label:screenN]`, converts screenshot-space pixels to physical screen coordinates using `CapturedScreen` metadata, and the overlay can fly to an explicit physical screen point

Keep artifact/debug plumbing that helps diagnose a single explicit point (`raw`, `annotated`, metadata, and `[POINT]` log lines), but simplify it so there is one source of truth for capture -> model response -> parsed directive -> converted screen point -> overlay dispatch timing
Do not delete the tray desktop smoke fixture or deterministic overlay/DPI tests; they remain useful for validating explicit coordinates and compositor last mile, not semantic AI localization

Restore the normal spoken-response path to one LLM request for the assistant answer; any `[POINT]` tag in that response may be parsed for baseline dispatch, but no additional AI coordinate call runs in this story
Add a regression test proving a pointing turn performs exactly one LLM request and does not wait on any verifier/locator before TTS can proceed
Add a short architecture note in `progress.txt` explaining what was removed and why: semantic target localization is unsolved, explicit coordinate conversion is separate and retained
Build: `dotnet build windows\Clicky.sln -c Release` succeeds. Tests: `dotnet test windows\Clicky.sln -c Release` succeeds
Commit all changes with message: `refactor: US-045 - Reset Windows pointing architecture`
userStories.notes: This is intentionally a cleanup/reset story, not a fix story. The current production behavior is wrong for Egypt/Algeria and slow. Do not preserve failed abstractions because they have tests. Preserve only the explicit coordinate parser/converter/overlay baseline and the artifact evidence needed for the rebuild.

Unit tests cover timing record formatting with fake clocks/fake providers; tests must not call real APIs
Manual run instructions are appended to `progress.txt`, including exactly what log lines to inspect before blaming Windows overlay or model vision

userStories.title: Design and document a fresh Windows pointing protocol
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a written design for how Windows pointing should work before code is rebuilt, so the implementation has clear contracts for semantic target selection, response speech, timing, fallback behavior, and artifact-based validation.
acceptanceCriteria: Create `windows/docs/pointing-redesign.md` describing the desired end-to-end protocol from user request -> screenshot capture -> model prompt -> response text -> point decision -> coordinate conversion -> overlay dispatch

Explicitly separate three concerns: semantic localization by a vision model, coordinate conversion from screenshot pixels to screen pixels, and visual overlay rendering. The doc must state which layer owns each failure mode

Compare at least three candidate protocols with tradeoffs: one-pass answer-with-POINT, two-phase point-first-then-answer, and structured JSON/tool-like response where speech text and point intent are separate fields

Define the target behavior for timing: if the user asks to show/point/click/find something, the cursor should appear as soon as a point decision is available and should not wait for long spoken prose or TTS completion

Define accuracy rules for hard semantic cases like world maps: point inside the visible country/region polygon or return no point; never point to a neighboring country, ocean, label-only guess, or unrelated semantic region
Define provider/model evaluation criteria and note whether Claude, GLM, or another configured model is acceptable for pixel-level semantic localization on Windows screenshots
Define the minimal implementation surface for the next story and explicitly list code that should remain out of scope until the protocol is proven
No production code changes except adding docs/tests that validate documented constants if useful
Commit all changes with message: `docs: US-047 - Design Windows pointing protocol`
userStories.notes: This story exists because repeated coding without a design produced garbage layers. Do the design work explicitly. It is acceptable for the conclusion to say the current provider cannot reliably do dense map pointing and must return `[POINT:none]` for those cases.
